exemplar.title: Math Mystery- A cognitive video game

detailedExemplarData.title: Math Mystery- A cognitive video game

strengths: Comprehensive research-questions table with credible, varied sources showcases strong information-literacy skills.
Thorough evaluation of multiple modeling and development tools, linking tool capabilities to project requirements.
weaknesses: ATL reflections are too general and not clearly aligned to your final choice of C# and C++, diluting the relevance of your examples.
Descriptions of thinking and research skills lack specific evidenceâfor instance, how particular tutorials or standards shaped your question design.
Self-management evidence is superficial; there is no documentation of dates met or how you adjusted your schedule in response to challenges.
Planner and code screenshots are unaccompanied by reflections on the ATL skills (e.g., debugging strategies) you applied to overcome specific technical issues.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: You demonstrate strong research and evaluation abilities, selecting and justifying tools effectively. To deepen your applying-skills section, tie each ATL skill explicitly to concrete decisions in C# and C++, support claims with detailed examples (such as tutorials that influenced your approach), and document how you managed your timeline and resolved coding challenges using specific ATL strategies.

strengths: Clear articulation of the intended educational game and its target audience, establishing a strong project focus.
Identification of multiple success-criteria categories, demonstrating an understanding of the projectâs key dimensions.
Use of an extended Gantt chart to outline a comprehensive, multi-month timeline for development.
weaknesses: Success criteria are not defined with measurable targets or mapped to specific performance levels.
Task-list timeline and short-term milestones lack explicit dates, resource allocations and task dependencies.
Absence of risk management or contingency planningâno buffer times or critical-path analysis included.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: Your planning section establishes a solid foundation by clearly defining the product, audience and an overarching schedule. However, it falls short in specifying how success will be measured at each stage, sequencing resources and dependencies, and anticipating potential delays. Incorporating quantifiable targets, detailed milestone dates with assigned tools or support, and a risk-management plan will make your plan more robust and feasible.

strengths: Insightful explanation of how learning C# and C++ has expanded your understanding and will inform future projects, reflecting genuine self-awareness and aspiration.
weaknesses: Reflections lack specific evidence linking skill development (critical thinking, self-management) to observable outcomes in your product.
Self-evaluation ratings are unsupported by user feedback or performance dataâno play-test results or metrics are cited.
You do not outline concrete action steps for further growth or evaluate the effectiveness of the solutions you implemented.
Challenges and solutions are described without assessing what worked well or what still needs improvement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: Your reflective narrative captures personal growth and forward-looking motivation, which is commendable. To strengthen this section, anchor your reflections in concrete examplesâinclude user feedback, cite metrics that validate your self-evaluation, and propose targeted steps to address remaining challenges. Evaluating the success of your solutions will close the loop on continuous improvement.

annotations.comment: Your JavaScript vs. Python comparison shows analytical skills, but itâs not aligned with your final language choice (C# and C++). Focus ATL reflections directly on your selected tools.
You list key math concepts but donât explain how thinking skills shaped question design. Connect each concept selection to evidence from student needs or curricular standards.

Your learning goal is clear, but you havenât defined measurable success criteria here. Specify how you will know youâve achieved each aspect of âunderstanding C# and C++â with concrete, quantifiable indicators.
You present a clear intended product and well-identified audience. This focus sets a solid foundation for defining detailed, sequenced steps to develop your educational game.

Youâve identified key success criteria categories, but they lack measurable targets (e.g., resolution, latency thresholds). Clarify specific metrics for aesthetics, functionality and educational effectiveness.
The success criteria table is useful but isnât mapped to your projectâs quantitative targets. Define which level (1â2, 3â4, etc.) you aim for with each criterion and explain your choice.
Your task-list timeline shows many activities but omits resource allocation and dependencies. Indicate required tools, personnel or learning materials and sequence tasks to ensure feasibility.
You describe skill development but donât identify areas for growth. Reflect on one key coding or design challenge and propose concrete steps to improve further.
You describe long-term goals clearly, but short-term milestones need dates and resource details. Add explicit deadlines and specify materials or support needed for each stage.
Your extended Gantt chart covers many months but lacks risk management or contingency planning. Highlight critical path tasks and propose buffer times to manage delays.
Your description of research skills is general. Provide specific examples of how particular sources influenced a decisionâe.g., how a Codecademy tutorial changed your language selection.
Youâve compiled a comprehensive research-questions table with credible sources. This demonstrates strong information-literacy skills in identifying relevant guidance for your coding choices.
You describe planning and deadlines broadly under self-management. Include specific evidenceâdates met, adjustments madeâto illustrate how you maintained your schedule.
You evaluated multiple modeling and development tools, demonstrating strong research and evaluation skills. Continue linking each toolâs capabilities to your specific project requirements.

Your planner screenshot shows systematic scheduling, but you need to connect planner tasks to concrete product milestonesâe.g., âCompleted level-design prototypeâ rather than general research tasks.
Your code screenshot illustrates product development, but reflect on the ATL skills you used (e.g., debugging strategies) to resolve a specific coding challenge shown in this excerpt.
You articulate how learning C# and C++ affected your understanding and future aspirations. Linking skill gains to future projects is a strong reflective practice.

You note enhanced critical thinking and self-management but donât link these reflections to specific evidence or outcomes. Cite an example where these skills directly impacted your productâs quality.
Your rationale for scoring shows insight, but you need to outline action steps to address areas scoring below targetâfor example, how will you enhance level complexity or interaction next time?
Your self-evaluation table rates aesthetics and effectiveness but lacks user feedback data. Incorporate survey or play-test results to triangulate your scores.
Your final evaluation addresses function and levels but misses direct evidence from players. Add actual performance metrics or quotes to support your ratings.
You outline challenges and solutions but donât evaluate their effectiveness. Reflect on what worked, what didnât, and how you adapted your approach.
Your reflection on product development interest is engaging, but connect that enthusiasm to specific learning outcomes or skill improvements demonstrated in the final game.
